title: OAuth 2.0 and OpenID Connect
description:
## Two Protocols That Work Together

People use "OAuth" and "OIDC" loosely, but they solve different problems.

| | **OAuth 2.0** | **OpenID Connect (OIDC)** |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | **Authorization** — delegated access to resources/APIs | **Authentication** — proving *who* the user is |
| Built on | — | An **authentication layer on top of OAuth 2.0** |
| Key artifact | **Access token** (for calling APIs) | Adds the **ID token** + the **UserInfo** endpoint |
| Answers | "Is this caller allowed to do X?" | "Who just logged in?" |

> OAuth 2.0 by itself was designed for *delegated authorization* — letting App A call App B's API on a user's behalf. It never standardized *how to tell App A who the user is*. **OIDC fills that gap**: it adds the **ID token** (a signed JWT of identity claims) and a **UserInfo** endpoint, turning OAuth into a proper login protocol. Keycloak implements both.

### Discovery: the well-known endpoint

Every realm publishes its OIDC configuration at:

```
https://<host>/realms/<realm>/.well-known/openid-configuration
```

Clients fetch this document to **auto-discover** all the protocol endpoints, so you rarely hard-code URLs. The document lists, among others:

| Endpoint | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **authorization_endpoint** | Where the browser is redirected to start login and (for the code flow) obtain an authorization code |
| **token_endpoint** | Exchange a code (or other grant) for access / ID / refresh tokens |
| **userinfo_endpoint** | Return identity claims for the user given a valid access token |
| **jwks_uri** | The realm's public signing keys (JWKS), used to verify token signatures |
| **end_session_endpoint** | Log the user out / terminate the SSO session |
| **introspection_endpoint** | Validate/inspect a token server-side (OAuth token introspection) |

```jsonc
// trimmed example of the discovery document
{
  "issuer": "https://sso.example.com/realms/acme",
  "authorization_endpoint": ".../protocol/openid-connect/auth",
  "token_endpoint":         ".../protocol/openid-connect/token",
  "userinfo_endpoint":      ".../protocol/openid-connect/userinfo",
  "jwks_uri":               ".../protocol/openid-connect/certs",
  "end_session_endpoint":   ".../protocol/openid-connect/logout"
}
```

> **Takeaway:** Use **OIDC** when you need to log a user in; rely on the **access token** (OAuth) when an API needs to authorize a call. In Keycloak you usually get both from a single login.
